clean_story:
Broken tools. Hidden answers.

The phrase how errors expose truths fits like a detective’s trick: mistakes act as flashlights in the dark, revealing what perfection hides.

A cracked mirror doesn’t just distort—it splits light into colors, proving white is a mix of wavelengths. A misprinted banknote’s ink smudge might uncover counterfeit dyes. In 1962, a failed satellite launch (Ariel 1) exposed gaps in Earth’s radiation belts, later mapped by working probes. Errors aren’t noise; they’re signals with the volume turned up.

Software bugs do this too. The 2012 Knight Capital glitch—$460 million lost in 45 minutes—revealed how high-frequency trading relied on untested code paths. The error wasn’t random; it was a stress test no designer dared run. Even DNA “typos” (mutations) expose evolutionary history: sickle-cell traits trace back to malaria resistance, a flawed defense that saved lives.

Three related subjects are failure-driven design, accidental scientific discoveries, and why lies need consistency.
